speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE SOUNDING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 5th of November, 1995. The survivor is Leon Wise. The interviewer is Larraine Rubinstein. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language of the interview today will be English. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Larraine Rubinstein. Today's date is November the 5th, 1995, and I'm conducting an interview with Leon Wise here in his home in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, please can you tell us your name and spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your full name and spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Leon Wise. And I was born in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In August, 1933, in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- the 25th of August, 1933. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a mixed up thing, you see. When my parents came from Poland in 1927-- migrated to France-- somehow, I don't know the reason why, but I took my mother's name. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Cybulska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Y-B-U-L-S-K-A. And at a later stage, I took my father's name, which was the proper thing to do. I don't know the reason why, but when I got my birth certificate, the proper name was this-- Wajselbaum. W-A-J-S-E-L-B-A-U-M. That's-- and I carried that name-- actually, both names-- through, through the war. I don't know the reason why. I think that because my parents were married in Poland religiously, not, not in a civil-- but then I investigated on my birth certificate and it gave me the proper name of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I have no idea why it became [INAUDIBLE]. I used the name of my mother Cybulska, you see? And you were born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dixieme arrondissement, which is tenth arrondissement. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH] And we lived in the 19th arrondissement. The 19th arrondissement, before the war, was like-- I wouldn't say a Jewish ghetto-- but it was-- most, most Jews lived around there in that part of Paris. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Simon-- which, is in English, is Simon. My mother's name was Edith, Edith. And I know very little about-- because I left when we separated because of the war. I was only barely six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you-- what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the-- he was in the leather type of business. He used to make shoes. He used to make bags. And I think he was also doing things with my uncle, my mother's brother, who is today, incidentally, still alive in Paris. He's 89 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Cybulski. Maurice Cybulski. And he has a son, my cousin. His name was Henry, Henry. And we see each other whenever we can. We speak to them on the phone and so on. And I'm very fortunate to have that, at least, left over. He's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories have you got of your father? What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very vaguely, I know that my father was-- well, of course, we were a very close family. And I was his-- I wouldn't say favorite, but because I was the youngest one-- and if I didn't eat, I remember he used to say to me, if you eat your meals, then I'll give you 20 centime and you can go down the road and buy some halva. Because I was mad on halva, even today. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? So being a Jewish quarter, we always found somebody who was making halva in their area. In-- near-- it was Belleville. Belleville is 19th arrondissement. Mhm. Yeah. And your family? Your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- right up to present time, we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their names and their ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is six years older than I am. Her name is Raymonde. She, she lives in Switzerland. She's married, and her name is Feuerwerker. And she has two, two girls, Shulamit and Beatrice. Beatrice lives in Geneva. Shulamit lives in London, and she has three children. And Beatrice has two children. My brother lives in Switzerland, as well. He has two, two boys. Two boys and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Henry, Henry. And he's 19 months older than I am. Yes. We, we speak on the phone quite often, and I saw him recently, in January. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have of the three of you at home before the war? Is there-- are there incidents that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- no. Very little, actually. Very little. The only type of memories I've got is really when we left home to go to this children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember outings, perhaps, with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just visiting, you know, parks in Paris. You know, that type of things, but-- and going to the Cheder, as well. We used to go to the Cheder. It was run by Rabbi Zalman Schneersohn, the original Schneersohn. Incidentally, the one who passed away in New York last year was his cousin. He was related to him. But Rabbi Schneersohn had a lot of impact with us once we are-- you know, till, till the end of the war. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what do you remember of the Cheder classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to have lots of fun. It used to be close to the river Seine. It was in rue de Dieu. And I remember that he used to also run a kosher restaurant. So my parents used to come every now and again when they-- you know, when my sister and my brother-- we used to go there together. And I can remember one-- in 1939, the river was frozen. The only time that the river Seine happens to be frozen. And my sister-- we went across-- we walked across. And it was this-- vaguely remember that. You know, that was fun. That's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember getting up to mischief as a child with your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember that. No. No. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were good boys, you see? [LAUGHS] Yes. Even friends-- family friends, yes. I had cousins. Also very vaguely that I can remember that. But very little of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of memories do you have of them? Any impressions? That-- not much impression, but we were together in-- going to the Cheder, visiting one another. Sliding down-- what do you call-- stair case. Because in Paris, there's a lot of-- up-- little, little roads, little streets with stair cases. So we used to slide down the banister. That type of things. It's, it's about all. And we used to go to a place called Buttes Chaumont. Yes. That was a-- one of the biggest park, park in, in Paris itself. Today it still exists. You know, that-- I used to go-- this I remember very well. We used to go on picnics and things like that. With the family or with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister and my brother. Of course-- of course, with my parents. Of course, yes. And my cousins. Yes. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe your house a little bit? Do you remember what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a sort of-- in a-- in an apartment. It was a cul-de-sac. And up on the first floor, we lived in the apartment. And of course, there was a concierge, like everywhere in Paris. But I have a very, very small recollection of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else living in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a-- an auntie lived upstairs on the fourth, fourth or fifth floor, which we saw during the war. And she died after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who looked after you as children? Was your mother looking after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother. Oh, yes. Yes. She was a hardworking lady. She used to-- she used to make a lot of clothes at home for us. And I remember, she had a sewing machine. In those days, I think everybody had, you know, a sewing machine. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I saw that particular machine after the war. But I'll tell you that in the sequence as it comes through with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from going to Cheder, did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was too small to go-- to go to school. I vaguely recollect, not far from where we were, there was a school I used to go. You know, like a kindergarten type of things. My mother used to walk me every now and again. And I can't remember exactly the street anymore. I know-- I know where it is. But I don't remember the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would she walk with you to the kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes. And with my brother. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was already at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was at school already, yes. Yes. In the girls' school. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born in '33, Hitler had already come to power. Would you have been aware, at that stage, that there was something uneasy? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only time I remember is that in 1939, when the war started, we'd hear the siren. So we used to go into the cellar. And we had gas masks, as well. You know, and-- for fear that they were going to bomb Paris. And it got worse and worse as time went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you conscious of Italy having invaded France?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, no. But this is when Rabbi Schneersohn assembled-- you know, spoke to my parents, and Spoke to hundreds of families. And asked them to assemble at, at the station in-- the station was Gare de Lyon-- to, to move into-- to get all the children to Vichy. When it got-- when we heard that they invaded the north of-- north of Europe or something, you know. The German came in and it's getting-- it was getting worse and worse, bombing and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, he was able to assemble all those children-- which we were a part of it-- to a children's home. And we ended up in Chateau Brout-Vernet-- Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet in Departement Allier. And it was-- I think it was about 40 or 60 kilometers from Vichy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was safe for us to be there. And it was a, a religious-- when I say religious, we didn't eat any treif at all. Although we didn't have too much food, anyway. But we never ate treif there at all. It was quite, quite strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early-- late 1939, beginning 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it was-- what it was like when you assembled, when you got together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can remember that we walked-- we walked down to-- I have a vivid memory with this. I was only, you know, as I said, five, six years old. And we hailed a taxi to go to Gare de Lyon with our suitcase. And we said goodbye to my parents. And we thought a long journey was fun. And we assembled with 120 boys and girls. So we were in a train to Brout-Vernet. And that's about the only time I remember that section. And then, that sort of fade away, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the things that your parents were saying to you when you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I couldn't remember. I thought we were going, maybe, on a holiday or something like that. That was my reaction. But I didn't realize that we were going away for good, you see? So-- and in the-- and in the home, we met friends of the family-- the children of the friends of the family. And I must say that I still see them today. When I go to France, we visit one another. Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember anything about the journey? About getting to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was very happy to be on the train. And this, this-- yes, I was quite happy. And not thinking that I would never come back. That's about it. And so you know, we made our life in Chateau des Morelles. It was a chateau which was given by the baron of [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a, a beautiful-- what you call-- a beautiful chateau, which was built in the 17th century. It was one, one square kilometer, the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had really a fantastic time. You know, even though-- and every now an again, my mother used to send us a letter or something like that. Or we had-- friends of the family used to come and visit. Once or twice, that's about it. And what news did you get of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: That everything was all right. And you know, my sister-- whatever my sister relayed to us. That's it. You know? And until-- it was in 1942 when my sisters took us in the corner somewhere in the-- in the garden in the home. And she told us that she was taken to, to Drancy, which was a camp, a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: From there, most of the people used to-- used to go to Auschwitz. Mhm. It was a rassemblement, you know? The famous, the famous Vel' d'Hiver, where they, they picked up all the Jews and put them into that, that area. You know, and so I remember that then I had tears in my eyes. I was very young, happy. And you know, when you're small, you don't realize, really, the-- all about it, you see? You know? Can you describe the house, the maison des Morelles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chateau des Morelles? Yes. Chateau des Morelles was a, a very big home. And it looked like-- almost like Buckingham Palace. And the corridors were-- the corridors were all wooden floors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And because we were so many-- so we were given task to clean the floors once a week. We used to get these-- what do you call these? To rub the, the floor with metal brush. And then polish, polish them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you were punished, that was-- you had to do that not only once or twice, but for one month you had to do it. So we had to be very careful. But it, it took-- this type of work, we didn't like-- we didn't like to do. But the home had to be looked after.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had, of course, eventually, rats, as big as rabbits practically, used to-- because we left a lot of food all over the place, you know? It was clean. But of course, being an old chateau-- so we had all the-- we used to try and catch them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Mhm. And how many children were there in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 120 boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what age group were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was six and seven by then. And up to the age of about 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the youngest, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one of the youngest, yes. I was one of the youngest. And we used to go to school. The school was two kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in France, you come back for lunch. And you go back after lunch and so on. And so in the morning, we used to walk in to rows of about four or five abreast. And we used to walk down to the village to Brout-Vernet, which was two kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: At lunchtime, we used to come back. After lunch, we used to go back to, to school. And at half past four, five o'clock, we used to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and-- in the winter time, it was absolutely terrible. Because by the time we reach in the-- on the road, we had to carry a wooden shovel to, to get it-- to get through this-- to get to the road to go to school. And that's one. And I can remember that, you know, being, being a country area, our school friends-- non-Jews-- used to bring a lot of food and they used to place them on shelves. So of course, we couldn't help ourselves but to-- well, let's say, to pinch them, you know? Because we were starving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, we used to have in the-- in this one square kilometer in our home, in the garden, we had fruit, fruit trees. So we always used to watch them growing and growing. Pears and apple. And they were barely ripe that we used to pinch them. And so on. And I'll never forget, also, that we used to have walnuts. So we found a step ladder. And we walked, walked up a bit and we put the ladder against the tree. And not knowing that downstairs, there was the farmer. It belonged, actually, to us, but he looked after the thing. He stood there waiting for us to come down. We got the shock of our lives when he-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --when he was there. He said, OK, you two. Carry the ladder. And you're punished, you see? So we walked in front. By the time he closed some gates, you see, we just dropped the ladder and then off we took.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they tried to, to find us in, in the dining room, when everybody used to be-- used to sit. And when-- walnuts, you know, when they still got the-- what do you call? That green-- before they're ripe, All your lips becomes brown. And your teeth. That was it. That's him. That's him. He used to pick us up like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say, that also, it was a religious home. So Shabbat was Shabbat with us. We used to do the benching. And on various-- on many occasions, I was asked to do the benching. And you know, it, it it was really-- that's where I got my, my, my training in Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH], really. So it was-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the children in school relate to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- there was a bit of anti-Semitism, definitely. Because we often used to fight. They used to call us-- in French, it's called, you know, dirty Jew or something like that. And of course, we used to gang-- they ganged up against us and we'd do the same thing. But that-- it was the norm in those days. And we had to be very careful, because we could be denounced. And it got-- it kept on getting worse and worse as time went by.

speaker: interviewer
text: We saw German patrol coming on horses in that area. And somehow, they didn't know there was a Jewish home. Or maybe-- I, I have an idea that the French police just kept them away from us, you see? And we were there till 1943. How, as a little boy of six and seven, were you aware that you shouldn't be seen by Germans or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they kept on telling us. And of course, you know, I think we learned that the Germans were, were-- well, when I found out that my parents were taken to a camp-- my, my father was taken-- was taken to camp, and the family. And we heard stories of other boys and girls, of their parents. So we realized we have to be very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were speaking Yiddish-- fluent Yiddish. And they told us not to speak Yiddish, to continue to, to speak French and to be very careful not to speak Yiddish when we used to go on excursions. Often, we used to go on excursions, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we didn't wear yarmulkes because-- we only wore beret, which is the norm in France. Of course, inside the dining hall, we'd wear our yarmulkes for eating. And I remember that sometimes-- I was quite friendly with Mr. Cogon, who used to gather the food. He used to go from farm to farm to get milk and supplies. And he had-- you know, he was driving to the farms to pick up the vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, we were assigned to pick up milk in these huge-- what do they call it? Those huge metal thing. And so we went-- half a dozen of us-- to pick up the milk. And of course, we sneaked in a-- a little cup. And by the time we got back, you see, there was quite a-- quite a bit missing. So we decided to fill it in with water. You see? So-- but that was the norm. Everybody used to do that, you know? And-- I mean, you know, looking back, we had a good time there. But of course, being small, we didn't realize, in many ways, what the outcome of it. You see? And there was a camaraderie?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a big-- yes. Yes. I can't remember having any fight-- I mean, there must have been fights, of course. But not to the extent that hated one another. I think that we helped one another. That was the thing. And I remember when I came there, originally, my mother had given me lots of shirts and things. And I was a little bit disappointed when the teacher, who looked after us said, OK. You have to give part of your shirts to so and so because he has none.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to share everything. In fact, we used to-- whenever you-- we used to eat an apple, a friend used to say, can you leave me the core? You know? I'll give you the core if you give me one of your slices of bread. We used to trade like that. And-- till the teacher found out. That was forbidden to do that sort of thing. But that was the norm amongst ourselves, you see? You know? Hm. And the teachers that were looking after you-- are you talking about teachers at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The teachers at home, you see? Being 120 boys and girls-- if I can remember, there was one director who was in charge, the headmaster. And there were-- we were-- we had so many groups. And I was assigned to a-- to a group. And it was run by Mr. and-- Monsieur et Madame Dybnis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dybnis. D-Y-B-N-I-S. Dybnis. And Miriam-- that was her first name-- and Henri. In fact, when Henri Dybnis came to our home, they got married. The two got married. It was a big wedding there. Well, not a big wedding. To us, it was a big wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they were like-- virtually like parents to us. You see? That was my group with them. And-- How many in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our group, we must have been about 20, 30 people. 30 boys and girls. And so what-- because you see, not all of us could go, let's say, on the excursion. [COUGHS] Excuse me. Because we were too many. Excuse me. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to make smaller group, of course. And we had to be careful. Because outside the home in the country, every now and again we would see a German patrol coming. Somehow, they were always on horses or walking, the German patrol, you see? So by the 1943, it got very-- it became very bad. In September. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in 19-- end of '39, '40.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a long period that you were in the one place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We stayed in Brout-Vernet right up to 19-- April, 1943. Somehow, we were notified-- the, the headmaster was notified that the Germans were coming to pick us up the following day. Please get out quickly. And so we left everything. And we split into various groups. My group was still with, with the Dybnis. And we went to-- we went to Grenoble, up in the mountains, au Chateau des Manoir. Chateau des Manoir was run by Rabbi Schneersohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chateau des Morelles--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chateau des Morelles, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your brother and sister in the same group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We always-- we never split. My brother and I, we always got-- we were always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And she was-- my sister was virtually like, like a mother to me. Because if I-- if I did the wrong thing, that -- I'd get the back of her hand. So I had to be very-- she was very strict with us. Looking back, it was a good idea, maybe, you know? But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, I think the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape one of the interview with Leon Wise. It's the 5th of November, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
